topic.what: Context-Fragmented Violations (CFVs) in LLM multi-agent systems where individual agent actions appear locally safe but collectively violate organizational policies due to siloed policy facts across departments
topic.how: Distributed Sentinel with Semantic Taint Token (STT) protocol using sidecar proxies for cross-domain policy verification while preserving data sovereignty
topic.why_matters: Existing prompt-based alignment and monolithic interceptors fail for violations spanning contextual islands; enterprise AI deployments require distributed enforcement above individual agents

characteristics.evidence: Table showing 0.95 F1 vs 0.85 and 0.65 baselines
characteristics.context: Compared against prompt-based filtering (0.85) and rule-based DLP (0.65)

characteristics.what_itasures: Total enforcement overhead from action initiation to decision
characteristics.context: 106ms total including 16ms verification plus 90ms entity extraction

characteristics.what_it_measures: Percentage of cross-domain flows causing policy violations across frontier LLMs
characteristics.evidence: Section 5.9 multi-agent violation study of 8 frontier LLMs
characteristics.context: GPT-5.4 GPT-4.1 Claude Opus 4.6 Gemini 2.5 Pro across 3 vendors

characteristics.what_itasures: F1 without Semantic Taint Token propagation
characteristics.context: Confirms STT propagation is essential component
160 attack cases 40 control across 9 categories 17 agent types

characteristics.what_itasures: Average length of inter-agent communication chains in benchmark

good_for: scenarios with distributed policy enforcement needs
requires: sidecar proxy infrastructure per agent domain

novelty: First formalization of Context-Fragmented Violations as distinct security threat class
Semantic Taint Token (STT) protocol for provenance-based taint tracking resistant to semantic laundering
PhantomEcosystem benchmark with 9 CFV categories and adversarially balanced controls
Theoretical proof (Theorem 2.6) that local enforcement cannot reliably detect CFVs without cross-domain information
Proof that provenance-based taint tracking is laundering-resistant unlike content-based approaches
open_problems: Constraints existing only in unstructured documents or tacit human knowledge fall outside formal model
Requires organizational policies to be codified in departmental knowledge graphs
Cross-domain query privacy guarantees need formal verification
Scaling to organizations with hundreds of agents and complex inter-departmental relationships
